Things I didn't like and observations while using:
1. When setting up, you need to login for the first time from within the hub after you save it. Recommendation would be to save the login screens of key apps (JIRA, Figma, etc.) into hubs and then access
2. I was thinking about notifications from applications and sites within the browser. However, I think it would make sense to configure notifications for those individually and not have browser notifications for now
3. Slack browser-based version was a bit slow to load compared to the application, but that's okay
4. Be prepared for a lot of 2-factor authentication if you're first setting up your Oasis work environment
5. While tagging Sites and adding them to hubs, I mainly added tags that were related to the name of the article or thing. For example, I did a "Grok" tag for "Grok"
6. I noticed that some sites like Grok and Notion do not load and may only be available on specific browsers
7. I found myself relying on the ai buddy a lot rather than remember where each site was saved

As I start my workday as a worker with 37+ tabs open upon launching the Oasis Browser, the day feels overhwelming. I think about what tasks I want to do first. I begin my day by checking my email and meetings for the day on Calendar. Then, I look at all my open tabs and feel daunted by the lack of organization. The first command I try to do to resolve this is "close all duplicate tabs"

AI response: "I've closed all duplicate tabs for you."

However, I still saw similar tabs still open such as: 
1. https://us05web.zoom.us/postattendee?mn=22P_K-DiY6HOoqWkYddHu01flIYmBXoHCpea.lfRgizEwPiZGoJZ3
2. https://us05web.zoom.us/postattendee?mn=b38vkvX3r3S3ECj6WUdP4yUNsQ0V5Ci-XZNJ.vRwc2lQvqLxV6Fm8

AI response: "I've closed all tabs related to Zoom."

However, not all tabs that begin with "https://us05web.zoom.us" were closed after doing that command

For example, I want to be able to say something like "I have multiple tabs open relating to AWS, Deepgram, Lambda functions, and Supabase. Create a group for these."

I want the result of that command to be the following: the browser creates a new tab group with tabs related to AWS, Deepgram, Lambda Functoins, and Supabse inside of it.

Currently, the behavior is the following: a new tab group named "AWS, Deepgram, Lambda, Supabase" is created with one tab (the Oasis Feedback tab) saved in it.

column10: There is already a feature in firefox called "suggest more of my tabs". It is available when you manually create a tab group by clicking and dragging tabs on top of each other. If you click "suggest more of my tabs", the browser analyzes the tabs you have open and suggests more to be added ot the group, and it also suggests a contextual name for the tab group.

column5:
I’d like to request a new Oasis feature called Semantic History Search.
Problem

    Users often struggle to find pages they know they visited before, especially when they can’t remember exact titles, URLs, or specific keywords.

    People tend to remember the idea or context of what they saw (“that article about beginner investing” or “the recipe with lemon and garlic”) rather than precise page names.

    As a result, digging through history or trying multiple vague searches can be slow, frustrating, and sometimes unsuccessful.

Desired user experience

    Users can type natural, fuzzy descriptions of what they remember (topics, purposes, or rough phrases) and quickly see the most relevant past pages surface from their history.

    The history search should feel like asking “Show me that site I used to compare HDMI monitors” instead of having to recall the exact site name or words in its title.

    This makes Oasis feel more like a true personal memory assistant for the web, helping users easily return to important research, tools, and resources—even when their memory of the specifics is incomplete.

As a user, I want to be able to use an ai command that triggers the new existing "splitview" (Add splitview) feature in Oasis. Firefox introduced a "splitview" feature to see two tabs side by side. I want to be able to control that feature and use it through the AI assistant.

In the past, we created a "splitview" command that opens the tabs in two new windows which are in a splitview, so that functionality should be called something else. "Splitview" commands should pertain to the existing splitview functionality that is native to firefox, where two tabs are arranged in a splitview within the same window

column5:
I’d like to request a new Oasis feature to make managing too many tabs much easier using tab groups.

A common scenario: there are a dozen tabs open for a single project—news articles, references, emails—and everything quickly becomes unwieldy. With this feature, Oasis would let users say something like, “Add all tabs to Research tab group,” and every open tab would be saved into a tab group called “Research” for later. All those pages would be neatly organized in one place, so the user can close the on-screen clutter with confidence. Later, they could reopen that tab group and have everything restored just as they left it.
